learn_section_blockchain_p1: A blockchain is a distributed, append-only ledger replicated across many nodes. Transactions are grouped into blocks; each block references the previous block's hash, creating an immutable chain.
learn_section_blockchain_p2: Key ideas: decentralization, consensus (Proof of Work / Proof of Stake), immutability, and cryptographic signatures.
learn_section_investing_p1: Investing in crypto requires research, risk management and diversification. Start small and understand the token's purpose, liquidity, and team. Use dollar-cost averaging (DCA) to reduce timing risk.
learn_bullet_hardware_wallets: Use hardware wallets for large holdings (self custody)
learn_section_defi_p1: DeFi are financial applications built on blockchains that allow lending, borrowing, trading and yield farming without intermediaries. Common components: AMMs, lending protocols, and stablecoins.
learn_section_defi_p2: Risks include smart contract bugs, impermanent loss, and oracle manipulation. Prefer audited protocols and understand how funds are secured.
learn_section_web3_p1: Web3 refers to decentralized applications (dApps) that run on blockchains. They remove single points of control and enable new UX like token-based governance and composable protocols.
learn_section_web3_p2: When using dApps, always verify the URL, connect with a secure wallet, and review transaction details before approving.
learn_section_custody_p1: Self custody means you control the private keys to your funds. Hardware wallets (Ledger, Trezor) are the safest for long-term storage. Software wallets (MetaMask, WalletConnect) are convenient but more exposed.
learn_bullet_use_passphrase: Use passphrases/seed encryption for extra security
learn_section_custody_p2: Consider splitting funds between cold storage (hardware) and hot wallets for daily use.
learn_section_bitcoin_p1: Bitcoin is the first and most well-known cryptocurrency. It introduced the concept of a decentralized digital currency secured by Proof of Work and a global network of miners.
learn_section_bitcoin_p2: Bitcoin is often used as a store of value; understand its supply cap, on-chain metrics, and the differences between Bitcoin and smart-contract platforms.
learn_section_wallets_p1: Wallets are interfaces that hold your keys and let you interact with blockchains. Software wallets (mobile/extension) are convenient for daily use; hardware wallets are the safest for long-term storage.
learn_section_wallets_p2: When choosing a wallet, consider security, recovery options, supported chains, and integration with dApps
learn_section_custody_diff: Differences: Bitcoin uses UTXO model and native Bitcoin wallets; EVM chains (Ethereum and compatibles) use account model and smart-contract based tokens (ERC-20). Custody on BTC focuses on managing private keys and UTXO selection, while EVM custody also includes approving contracts and managing token allowances.
learn_section_custody_bluewallet_steps_p: BlueWallet is a mobile Bitcoin wallet that supports on-device key management. To self-custody with BlueWallet: create a new wallet, securely back up the seed phrase, optionally enable passphrase, and use the built-in on-chain tools to receive and send BTC.
learn_section_investing_p2: Exchanges let you buy/sell crypto (centralized exchanges - KYC) and you can withdraw to your personal wallet for self custody. P2P marketplaces allow direct trades between users. Always move funds from exchanges to your wallet for long-term storage and verify addresses carefully before withdrawing.
